---
title: "Models and Providers"
description: "Choose AI Gateway or a direct AI SDK provider, configure credentials, and set context limits."
---

We recommend Vercel AI Gateway for most agents. A `provider/model` string keeps routing and
credentials separate from the agent code and gives eve the model metadata it needs for compaction.
Use a direct AI SDK provider when you need provider-specific routing, direct billing, or a model the
Gateway does not offer.

The `model` field in `agent/agent.ts` makes that choice: a string uses Gateway, while an AI SDK
model object calls the provider directly.

## Vercel AI Gateway

A gateway model uses a `provider/model` string:

```ts title="agent/agent.ts"
import { defineAgent } from "eve";

export default defineAgent({
  model: "anthropic/claude-sonnet-4.6",
});
```

On Vercel, project OIDC can authenticate the request. Elsewhere, set `AI_GATEWAY_API_KEY` or a
valid `VERCEL_OIDC_TOKEN`. The initializer writes the model selected during setup; inspect
`agent/agent.ts` instead of assuming a fixed scaffold default.

Use `/model` in the local terminal UI to select a catalog model and configure Gateway access.

## Direct provider

> **Recommendation:** Stay on Gateway unless direct provider control solves a concrete requirement.
> With a direct provider, you own the provider package, credential, model identifier, and context
> metadata for unlisted models.

Install the provider package you import. It is not bundled with eve:

```bash
npm install @ai-sdk/anthropic
```

Then pass the provider model object and set the provider's credential:

```bash
export ANTHROPIC_API_KEY=...
```

```ts title="agent/agent.ts" check
import { anthropic } from "@ai-sdk/anthropic";
import { defineAgent } from "eve";

export default defineAgent({
  model: anthropic("claude-sonnet-4-6"),
});
```

This request goes directly to Anthropic; it does not pass through AI Gateway. The same pattern works
with any AI SDK-compatible provider.

## Unlisted and compatible providers

eve needs the model's context-window size to decide when to compact a long session. Gateway catalog
models provide that metadata. For a custom, self-hosted, OpenRouter, LiteLLM, or otherwise unlisted
model, set it explicitly:

```ts title="agent/agent.ts"
import { createOpenAICompatible } from "@ai-sdk/openai-compatible";
import { defineAgent } from "eve";

const provider = createOpenAICompatible({
  name: "internal",
  apiKey: process.env.INTERNAL_MODEL_API_KEY,
  baseURL: "https://models.example.com/v1",
});

export default defineAgent({
  model: provider("company-model"),
  modelContextWindowTokens: 128_000,
});
```

Use the provider's documented context limit. An inflated value delays compaction past the real model
limit; a value that is too small compacts more often than necessary.

## Provider options

`modelOptions.providerOptions` is forwarded to the AI SDK model call:

```ts title="agent/agent.ts"
import { defineAgent } from "eve";

export default defineAgent({
  model: "anthropic/claude-sonnet-4.6",
  modelOptions: {
    providerOptions: {
      anthropic: {
        thinking: { type: "enabled", budgetTokens: 4_000 },
      },
    },
  },
});
```

Provider option names and values belong to the selected AI SDK provider. eve forwards them but does
not validate their semantics.

## Calls, limits, and cost

One user turn is not necessarily one model request. A tool loop can make another request after every
batch of tool results. Subagents have their own loops. Compaction can make a summary request, and
LLM-backed eval assertions call their judge model separately. Provider rate limits and cost should
therefore be sized for steps, not only user messages.

`step.completed` stream events report token usage for each model step. Use
[Observability](../operate/observability) to export traces and [Evals](../operate/evals) to measure
behavior before changing models.

Review the selected provider's terms, retention policy, and data controls before sending production
or regulated data.
